sources: Avoma discovery call 20 May 2026, Mona C Y Chiu (is_rep:false)
Avoma notes: 'Lack of single source of truth for procurement data; dashboards and analysis are difficult to build' (20 May)
Avoma notes: 'End users must review extensive policy documents to determine required PR documentation' (20 May)
metrics.narrative: Business problem is clearly articulated but not yet quantified. The buyer stated on the 20 May discovery call: 'lack of single source of truth for procurement data; dashboards and analysis are difficult to build' (Mona, is_rep:false). Additional pain: disparate systems (Oracle P2P basic fulfillment, old in-house Microsoft forms for eTendering, repository-only CLM, no sourcing system, outdated supplier onboarding) create coverage gaps, compliance risk, and poor user experience. End users must 'read through a lot of policy document to determine what kind of documents' are required for a PR (20 May call). No quantified savings target, cycle-time reduction, or headcount impact has been stated. The buyer's concern about partner-driven cost inflation ('partners modifying off-the-shelf solutions for profit') implies budget sensitivity but no hard number. Making the business case quantitative (savings, cycle time, compliance improvement) is a ranked move.

sources: Contact Role: Mona C Y Chiu, Decision Maker (Procurement Executive)
Avoma discovery call 20 May 2026: Mona drove call, articulated pain/requirements, 'quite impressed' by AI demo
Next Step 6 Jun 2026: '75% confidence', 'well positioned with Mona', 'high propensity for AI', 'need to navigate exec levels asap'
Evidence gap: no executive introductions yet, no advocacy evidence beyond RFI submission, single-threaded
champion.narrative: Champion is developing but not yet strong. Mona C Y Chiu (Procurement Executive, Decision Maker role) is the single engaged buyer contact and is driving the RFI evaluation. On the 20 May discovery call, Mona clearly articulated pain, requirements, and decision criteria, and stated she was 'quite impressed' by the relevant stakeholder demo at a prior event. The Next Step (6 Jun) describes the relationship as 'well positioned with Mona' with '75% confidence' and 'high propensity for AI', indicating positive sentiment and alignment. However, champion strength is developing, not strong, because: (1) Mona has not yet introduced Zycus to executive levels (Economic Buyer, CFO, or Head of Strategic Procurement), which the deal owner flags as a gap ('need to navigate exec levels asap'); (2) no evidence of active advocacy (sharing internal docs, coaching on the decision process, pushing back on competitors) beyond the RFI submission; (3) single-threaded: no other buyer stakeholders have engaged, creating ghost risk if Mona's priorities shift or she loses internal sponsorship. The Next Step indicates Carl (deal owner) is to 'follow up and meet Mona F2F in HK' (early June, now past), suggesting the relationship-building is still in flight. Developing Mona into a strong champion (securing her advocacy for exec introductions and process coaching) and multi-threading beyond her are the top moves.

sources: Avoma discovery call 20 May 2026, Mona: 'How do you compare with your solution with Coupa in terms of commercial?'
Avoma 20 May, Carl Kimball: 'Coupa is very rigid', 'don't wanna do any customizations', 'Coupa will only use partners', Zycus '85-90% fit, 5-6% configuration'
Avoma 20 May, Mona: 'partners twist the off-the-shelf solution for profit', 'we want to experience the real one' (prefers vendor-direct)
Avoma 20 May, Mona: impressed by Zycus AI demo at prior event, wants AI-driven off-the-shelf modular procurement
competition.narrative: Competition is confirmed: Coupa is the primary known competitor. On the 20 May discovery call, Mona C Y Chiu directly asked: 'How do you compare with your solution with Coupa in terms of commercial?' (verbatim quote, 20 May). Carl Kimball (Zycus deal owner) responded by positioning Zycus as more flexible/configurable than Coupa ('Coupa is very rigid', 'don't wanna do any customizations', 'what you see is pretty much that's it') and emphasized Zycus's middle-ground approach (85-90% off-the-shelf fit, 5-6% configuration, rest change management) vs Coupa's zero-customization model. Carl also noted Coupa uses partners-only for implementation, which aligns with Mona's stated concern about partner-driven cost inflation and solution twisting. The wedge: Zycus offers modular, flexible, configurable S2P with vendor-direct implementation and strong AI (agentic AI demo impressed the buyer), vs Coupa's rigid, partner-only model. Coupa's threat level is medium: the buyer is doing commercial comparison, but Mona's stated preferences (flexible, modular, vendor-direct, AI-friendly, configuration over customization) align more closely with the relevant stakeholder other competitors named. The incumbent stack (Oracle P2P basic fulfillment, in-house forms, repository CLM) is being displaced, not defended. The buyer is also evaluating 'do nothing' risk (continuing with disparate systems), but the RFI submission and pain articulation suggest strong intent to move. Recommended move: reinforce the Coupa wedge (modular flexibility, vendor-direct implementation, agentic AI leadership) in the RFI response and F2F meeting.

sources: Avoma discovery call 20 May 2026, Mona C Y Chiu (Decision Maker, Procurement Executive)
Avoma notes: 'Lack of single source of truth for procurement data; dashboards and analysis are difficult to build' (20 May)
Avoma notes: 'No sourcing system in place and outdated supplier onboarding process leads to coverage gaps' (20 May)
Avoma notes: 'Current CLM only serves as a repository; lacks authoring, clause libraries, and AI-driven capabilities' (20 May)
Avoma notes: 'End users must review extensive policy documents to determine required PR documentation' (20 May)
Avoma notes: 'Concern about partners modifying off-the-shelf solutions for profit, leading to a desire for authentic experiences instead' (20 May)
identify_pain.narrative: Pain is clearly identified and owned by the Decision Maker (Mona C Y Chiu, Procurement Executive). On the 20 May discovery call, Mona articulated: (1) 'lack of single source of truth for procurement data; dashboards and analysis are difficult to build' due to disparate systems (Oracle P2P basic fulfillment, in-house Microsoft forms, repository-only CLM); (2) no sourcing system in place, creating a functional gap; (3) outdated supplier onboarding process with coverage gaps and no automated risk evaluation or third-party database integration; (4) poor end-user experience: 'end users must review extensive policy documents to determine required PR documentation', leading to compliance risk and friction; (5) current CLM 'only serves as a repository; lacks authoring, clause libraries, and AI-driven capabilities'; (6) concern about partner-driven solution customization and cost inflation ('partners twist the off-the-shelf solution for profit'). The pain owner is Mona (Procurement Executive, Decision Maker role), who is driving the RFI and evaluation. The broader procurement and finance organization (Head of Strategic Procurement, CFO) likely share the pain but have not yet engaged. The buyer's stated desire for AI-driven, off-the-shelf, modular, and easy-to-adopt procurement directly addresses these pains. Pain is confirmed; quantifying the operational/financial impact (cycle time, error rate, savings) is the next step.

sources: Avoma discovery call 20 May 2026: no contracting/legal discussion
Avoma 20 May: data hosting question, geopolitical/sanction concern (Mona)
Avoma 20 May: 'governance control is very important to us' (Mona)
Avoma 20 May: integration with Oracle, CAMS, Maximo 'must be retained' (Mona)
paper_process.narrative: Paper process (contracting, legal, InfoSec, procurement channel) is unknown. No discussion of MSA, SOW, legal review, InfoSec questionnaire, or procurement approval mechanics on the 20 May discovery call. The buyer did ask about data hosting location and mentioned geopolitical sensitivity ('avoid western technology', concern about sanctions), which implies InfoSec and data sovereignty will be part of the review, but no formal InfoSec process or timeline was stated. The buyer's organization (Hong Kong Jockey Club) is a large, governance-focused entity ('governance control is very important to us'), suggesting a formal legal and compliance review is likely. Integration with Oracle, CAMS, and Maximo was flagged as 'must be retained' (20 May call), which may trigger IT/systems integration review. No mention of whether SI/partner will be involved in contracting (though the buyer prefers vendor-direct implementation). No Current_Contract_Expiration__c or contract-renewal forcing function. The paper process gap: legal review steps, InfoSec questionnaire and approval, IT integration sign-off, and any procurement/finance approval thresholds. This will surface after shortlist/vendor selection.

sources: Next Step 6 Jun 2026: 'need to navigate exec levels asap'
Avoma discovery call 20 May 2026: no budget/commercial authority discussion
Account-level contacts: David Fan (CFO), Jacqueline Lam Chu Wan (Head of Strategic Procurement) — not yet engaged on this opp
economic_buyer.narrative: Economic Buyer is not yet identified. No buyer participant on the 20 May discovery call discussed budget authority, commercial approval, or CFO/C-level sign-off. The Decision Maker (Mona C Y Chiu, Procurement Executive) is driving the RFI and evaluation but has not indicated she controls the commercial decision. The Next Step (6 Jun) flags 'need to navigate exec levels asap', signaling that exec sponsorship is recognized as a gap by the deal owner. Account-level contacts include the CFO (David Fan, Executive Director of Finance, Procurement and Investment Office) and Head of Strategic Procurement (Jacqueline Lam Chu Wan), both plausible Economic Buyer candidates, but neither has been engaged on this opportunity. The 20 May call included no pricing or budget discussion beyond modular flexibility and a comparison question about Coupa commercials. Mapping the Economic Buyer (likely CFO or Head of Strategic Procurement) and securing their active sponsorship is the highest-leverage move.

sources: Next Step 6 Jun 2026: 'RFI submitted', 'need to navigate exec levels asap', 'ck to follow up and meet Mona F2F in HK'
Avoma discovery call 20 May 2026: Mona driving, no other buyer stakeholders on call
Avoma 20 May: 'timeline is quite challenging' (Mona)
decision_process.narrative: Decision process is partially visible. The buyer is running an RFI-led evaluation: RFI was finalized and submitted to Zycus by early June 2026 (Next Step: '1st June- RFI finalizing by 2nd June', '6th June- RFI submitted'). The deal owner noted the need to 'navigate exec levels asap' (6 Jun Next Step), signaling that executive sponsorship (Economic Buyer / CFO / C-level) is the next gate and not yet secured. The 20 May discovery call shows the Decision Maker (Mona C Y Chiu) is driving the evaluation, but no other buyer stakeholders have appeared. No mention of a formal evaluation committee, shortlist timeline, or approval chain on the call. The buyer asked about Coupa commercial comparison and vendor vs partner implementation preference, suggesting comparative evaluation is underway. The timeline flags 'quite challenging' (20 May call), and the close date (30 Nov 2026) implies a 5-6 month decision cycle from RFI submission (Jun) to close (Nov), which is credible for an enterprise S2P deal. The process gap: who else is on the evaluation team, what is the shortlist/finalist timeline, and what is the approval chain (CFO, C-level, board). The Next Step's 'meet Mona F2F in HK' and 'navigate exec levels' are the process moves in flight.

sources: Avoma discovery call 20 May 2026, Mona C Y Chiu
Avoma notes: 'AI must be user-friendly while preserving governance and control to prevent compliance loss' (20 May)
Avoma notes: 'System should conduct supplier risk evaluations and integrate third-party databases to highlight risk factors' (20 May)
Avoma notes: 'We come across some experiences. We use a partner. They sometimes twist the off the shelf solution. We don't wanna see that. We want to experience the real one.' (20 May)
Next Step 6 Jun 2026: RFI submitted, detailed document
Products__c: full S2P suite (iContract, iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, eInvoicing, iRequest, iRisk)
decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria are clearly articulated from the 20 May discovery call and the RFI submission. The buyer is evaluating on: (1) off-the-shelf, modular solution (not heavy customization) with flexible cost model to allow phased implementation (source-to-contract first, potentially expanding to full S2P); (2) AI-driven user experience (guided PR creation, policy compliance automation) that is 'easy to adopt' while preserving governance and control ('AI doesn't mean control becomes looser'); (3) strong governance across 6 diverse procurement categories (construction requires tight governance, F&B and marketing require agility); (4) integration with Oracle P2P/finance, CAMS (F&B supplier ordering), and Maximo (facility service orders); (5) end-to-end S2P platform including sourcing (currently missing), modern supplier onboarding with third-party risk evaluation and database integration, and a CLM with authoring, clause libraries, and AI (current CLM is repository-only); (6) single source of truth for procurement data with easy dashboarding and self-service analytics; (7) vendor implementation preferred over partner (buyer voiced concern about partner cost inflation and solution twisting). The RFI document is described as detailed across these areas. Configuration over customization is a stated preference. Zycus positioning: 85-90% fit off-the-shelf, 5-6% configuration, rest via change management (stated on call). Geopolitical sensitivity noted: 'avoid western technology' due to sanction risk, but Singapore data center is acceptable (20 May call).

ai_fit_signal.summary: AI Hungry. Buyer explicitly impressed by Zycus agentic AI demo, wants AI-driven guided PR creation and policy compliance automation, and Next Step (6 Jun) confirms 'high propensity for AI'. Buyer articulated AI governance requirement (easy adoption, preserved control/compliance), which Zycus addressed on 20 May call. AI is a top decision criterion and a wedge against Coupa (Coupa lacks agentic AI leadership). Calls show AI appetite is accurately rated.

items.change: Opportunity created, discovery call held with Decision Maker (Mona C Y Chiu), pain and requirements articulated, RFI process initiated
RFI submitted to Zycus, Next Step updated: 75% confidence, well positioned with Decision Maker, need to meet F2F in HK and navigate exec levels

deal_movement.summary: Deal is live and progressing. Created 20 May 2026, discovery call same day (20 May). RFI finalized by buyer 2 Jun, submitted to Zycus 6 Jun. Next Step updated 6 Jun with 75% confidence, noting RFI submitted, need for F2F meeting in Hong Kong with Decision Maker (early June, now past), and need to navigate exec levels. Last Salesforce activity 14 Jun (12 days ago). No recent rep-initiated outreach logged beyond the 6 Jun Next Step. Deal is in Formal Evaluation stage (healthy for 37 days old). No forward slip. Buyer is engaged (RFI submission, detailed discovery call). Primary gap: exec-level engagement (Economic Buyer) not yet secured, single-threaded.

items.detail: Economic Buyer not yet identified or engaged. Next Step (6 Jun) flags 'need to navigate exec levels asap'. CFO (account contact) or Head of Strategic Procurement are plausible Economic Buyers, but neither has engaged on this opportunity. Without exec sponsorship, commercial approval and budget sign-off are at risk.

Single-threaded: only Decision Maker (Procurement Executive) has engaged. No other buyer stakeholders (Evaluator is dormant, no exec or IT contacts on opp). If the Decision Maker's priorities shift or she loses internal sponsorship, deal is at ghost risk. Multi-threading is urgent.

Buyer asked direct commercial comparison with Coupa on 20 May call, signaling price sensitivity. Buyer also voiced concern about partner-driven cost inflation. Modular pricing and flexible cost model (source-to-contract first, phased expansion) are stated requirements. If Zycus pricing is not competitive and modular, buyer may default to Coupa brand or status quo.

Buyer stated timeline is 'quite challenging' on 20 May call. Close date is 30 Nov 2026 (157 days out), which is credible for a 5-6 month RFI-to-close cycle, but depends on securing exec engagement and moving to shortlist within 60 days. If exec engagement or RFI response delays, close date may slip.

Geopolitical/data sovereignty sensitivity: buyer stated 'we try to avoid western technology' due to sanction risk. Singapore data center is acceptable (Carl confirmed 20 May), but this may surface as a concern in InfoSec review or exec-level discussion. Zycus being a private company (not US public) mitigates somewhat.

reasons.detail: This $350K Formal-Evaluation deal (win 44, mom 58) has had zero activities, movements, or calls in the last 14 days and 22 days since any touch, with the next step unchanged since 6 June. The RFI was submitted and a F2F with Decision Maker Mona Chiu in Hong Kong was the plan, but momentum has stalled and the Economic Buyer (likely CFO David Fan or Head of Strategic Procurement Jacqueline Lam Chu Wan) is still unmapped. If the silence continues, Coupa can close the exec gap first and we risk slipping a $350K deal that rests on a single-threaded developing champion.
reasons.metric: 22 days no activity on a $350K deal; next step unchanged since 6 Jun

items.expected_effect: Creates quantified business case (savings, cycle time, compliance improvement) for Economic Buyer and CFO approval. Buyer stated concern about partner cost inflation, signaling budget sensitivity. Quantified ROI is required gate for Shortlisted and Vendor Selected stages.

confidence_signals.summary: Strong confidence signals. RFI submitted 6 Jun (detailed, buyer-led). Discovery call 20 May showed engaged Decision Maker with clear pain articulation and positive sentiment toward Zycus AI. Next Step 6 Jun indicates 75% confidence, well positioned with Decision Maker, high AI propensity. No cooling signals. Primary gap: exec engagement (Economic Buyer) not yet secured, single-threaded. Last Salesforce activity 12 days ago (14 Jun), within healthy window for this stage. Deal is live and on track.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 20 May 2026, 37 days old. In Formal Evaluation 37 days (healthy for this stage; typical RFI-to-shortlist is 60-90 days). Close date 30 Nov 2026 is 157 days out. Discovery call 20 May (~5 weeks ago), RFI submitted 6 Jun (~3 weeks ago), Next Step updated 6 Jun. Last Salesforce activity 14 Jun (12 days ago). No forward slip recorded. Pace required: move to Shortlisted within 60 days, then vendor selection and commercials through Q3 2026, contracting Q4 2026. Timeline is credible and buyer is engaged.

best_practice_check.summary: Three substantive win-strategy levers for this deal

competitive_position.summary: Coupa is the single known active competitor, medium threat. Mona asked direct commercial comparison on 20 May discovery call. Coupa's rigid, partner-only model is misaligned with buyer's stated preferences (modular, flexible, vendor-direct, AI-friendly, configuration over customization). Zycus positioned as the better fit: modular S2P, 85-90% off-the-shelf with configuration flexibility, vendor-direct implementation, agentic AI leadership (buyer impressed at prior event). Incumbent stack (Oracle P2P basic, in-house forms, repository CLM) is being displaced, not defended. No other competitors named. Zycus is well positioned if we reinforce the wedge (modular flexibility, vendor-direct, AI) and map the Economic Buyer before Coupa does.

competitors.name: Coupa
competitors.quote: How do you compare with your solution with Coupa in terms of commercial? (Mona C Y Chiu, 20 May 2026)
competitors.how_we_win: Emphasize modular flexibility (source-to-contract first, expandable), vendor-direct implementation (vs Coupa partner-only), configuration capability (vs Coupa rigid off-the-shelf), and agentic AI leadership (Merlin for guided PR creation, policy compliance automation). Reinforce in RFI response and F2F meeting.

competitors.name: Do Nothing / Incumbent Stack
competitors.quote: Lack of single source of truth for procurement data; dashboards and analysis are difficult to build. No sourcing system in place and outdated supplier onboarding process leads to coverage gaps. Current CLM only serves as a repository. (Mona, 20 May 2026)
competitors.how_we_win: Buyer pain is clear and quantified (disparate systems, poor user experience, compliance risk). RFI submission signals strong intent to move. Risk is low unless budget or exec sponsorship stalls.

items.commitment: Provide modular pricing breakdown with indicative pricing and flexible cost model options (source-to-contract vs full S2P)
items.deliverable: Provide modular pricing breakdown with indicative pricing and flexible cost model options (source-to-contract vs full S2P)
items.grounding_quote: Provide a modular breakdown with indicative pricing and flexible cost model options for a modular implementation (e.g., source-to-contract vs. full source-to-pay). (Avoma action item, 20 May 2026). I will give you a modular breakdown, and also rough indication, and if we bundle all modules we can give you significant concession or discount. (Carl, 20 May 2026)

items.commitment: Deliver detailed presentation on Zycus implementation approach (85-90% fit, configuration, change management) and customer KPIs/outcomes
items.deliverable: Deliver detailed presentation on Zycus implementation approach (85-90% fit, configuration, change management) and customer KPIs/outcomes
items.grounding_quote: I'd love to take you through a presentation that explains all of that in detail. I would love to come and visit you guys and maybe do a presentation of what are our customers like you doing, what benefit are they getting, outcomes, especially some of the KPIs, pretty exciting savings. (Carl, 20 May 2026)

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Strong AI positioning. Buyer has high AI appetite: impressed by Zycus agentic AI demo at prior event, explicitly wants AI-driven guided PR creation and policy compliance automation, and Next Step (6 Jun) flags 'high propensity for AI'. Buyer also voiced governance concern: AI must be easy to adopt while preserving control and compliance ('AI doesn't mean control becomes looser'). Zycus positioned AI governance and guided workflows on 20 May call. AI is a key differentiator and decision criterion. Make Merlin agentic AI (guided PR creation, policy automation across 6 diverse procurement categories) the centerpiece of the RFI response and F2F meeting.

items.quote: We are quite impressed for the demonstration about the AI, you know, how to help us to raise a request. We would like to explore more. We're looking for something off the shelf modularized procurement system. (Mona, 20 May 2026)
items.criterion: Off-the-shelf, modular S2P with AI-driven user experience (guided PR creation, policy compliance automation)

items.quote: I would like to see the cost model is flexible for modular implementation because maybe eventually we just want to have the source of contract instead of source of pay. (Mona, 20 May 2026). Carl confirmed: we're happy to give you modular, and we can phase the implementation in any way you want. (20 May 2026)
items.criterion: Flexible cost model for phased/modular implementation (source-to-contract first, potentially expanding to full S2P)

items.quote: We would like to see more you can do for our configuration rather than customization. (Mona, 20 May 2026). Carl: We average 85-90% fit, configuration will take another 5-6%, then change management to 100% fit. (20 May 2026)

We come across some experiences. We use a partner. They sometimes twist the off the shelf solution for profit. We don't wanna see that. We want to experience the real one. (Mona, 20 May 2026). Carl confirmed Zycus can do vendor-direct implementation. (20 May 2026)

There's some integration we require with our system like the CAMS and Maximo. This must be retained. (Mona, 20 May 2026). Carl: We have quite a few customers in Asia who run Maximo, we have good experience with that. (20 May 2026)
items.criterion: Integration with Oracle P2P/finance, CAMS (F&B supplier ordering), Maximo (facility service orders)

items.quote: For the approval, the management perspective, with the AI, doesn't mean the control become looser. You have to show me your AI is very easy to adopt but the governance or the control will not be losing. (Mona, 20 May 2026). Carl positioned Zycus guided workflows and compliance-preserving AI. (20 May 2026)
items.criterion: AI with governance and control (AI adoption doesn't compromise compliance)

items.quote: We don't have a single source of truth of data. Every single time we make procurement decision, maybe not 100% rely on the data. We would like to have a system very easy to create our own dashboard or to do some analysis. (Mona, 20 May 2026). Carl: Our platform has repository for one source of truth, 3D drill-down reporting and cubes, we can build dashboards, very straightforward. (20 May 2026)
items.criterion: Single source of truth for procurement data with easy dashboarding and self-service analytics

items.quote: We need to pick up the technology very careful because we try to avoid western technology because of geopolitical reason. We don't want to be one of the sanctions. (Mona, 20 May 2026). Carl: Our data center is in Singapore, and we could look at Hong Kong if needed. (20 May 2026)
items.criterion: Geopolitical/data sovereignty sensitivity (avoid western technology, sanction risk)

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Strong fit across all stated criteria. Buyer wants: (1) off-the-shelf modular S2P with AI — Zycus full suite (iContract, iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, eInvoicing, iRequest, iRisk) with agentic AI demo already impressed buyer; (2) flexible cost model for phased implementation — Zycus positioned modular pricing and phased rollout on 20 May call; (3) configuration over customization — Zycus 85-90% fit, 5-6% config aligns; (4) vendor-direct implementation — Zycus offers this, buyer voiced concern about partner inflation; (5) integration with Oracle, CAMS, Maximo — Zycus experienced, Carl confirmed on call; (6) AI with governance/control — Zycus positioned this directly; (7) single source of truth with easy dashboarding — Zycus reporting/analytics capability confirmed. One exposure: geopolitical sensitivity (avoid western tech, sanction risk), but Singapore data center is acceptable and Zycus is private (not US public company subject to sanctions). Overall: aligned.

hard.ais_why:
Signal	Weight	Finding	Score
Initial AI Trigger	High	Attended Zycus event specifically because of the AI demo for raising requests 	+2.5
AI Adoption Openness	Medium	Acknowledges AI can speed processes and improve UX — receptive in principle 

+1.5
Governance Concerns	High	Explicitly worried AI will loosen governance controls; needs proof it won't 	−1.5
Category Complexity	Medium	6 procurement categories with different governance needs adds AI implementation complexity 	−1.0
AI Categorization by Rep	Medium	Carl noted Zycus categorizes customers by AI interest; HKJC is not explicitly high-AI 	Neutral
No AI-Specific Feature Asks	Low	No proactive requests for AI-specific features beyond governance/UX guidance	−0.5
RFI Omission	Low	RFI had little detail on AI expectations, suggesting it's not a top-of-mind priority 

−0.5

deltas.subject: Multi-thread beyond the Decision Maker immediately. This deal is single-threaded: only Mona (Decision Maker, Procurement Executive) has engaged. The account has 20+ procurement, finance, and IT contacts (including CFO, Head of Strategic Procurement, multiple Strategic Procurement Managers, IT heads), but none are on this opportunity's contact roles beyond Mona and one Evaluator (Lois W C Ho, Senior Manager, no engagement evidence). Single-threading creates ghost risk if Mona's priorities shift, she loses internal sponsorship, or another vendor builds a coalition. The Next Step's 'navigate exec levels' is the entry point: use the F2F meeting and RFI response to request introductions to the CFO, Head of Strategic Procurement, and the IT/integration stakeholders (for Oracle/CAMS/Maximo fit validation). Add these contacts to the opportunity contact roles as they engage, and track multi-thread coverage as a leading indicator.

deltas.to: Map and engage the Economic Buyer (CFO David Fan or Head of Strategic Procurement Jacqueline Lam Chu Wan) immediately. This is a $350k, governance-heavy deal at
deltas.subject: Map and engage the Economic Buyer (CFO David Fan or Head of Strategic Procurement Jacqueline Lam Chu Wan) immediately. This is a $350k, governance-heavy deal at a large enterprise buyer (Hong Kong Jockey Club). The Decision Maker (Mona, Procurement Executive) is engaged and driving the RFI, but the Next Step (6 Jun) explicitly flags 'need to navigate exec levels asap', and no exec-level buyer has engaged yet. The Economic Buyer will control the commercial decision and budget sign-off. Carl (deal owner) should request the Decision Maker to introduce Zycus to the CFO or Head of Strategic Procurement within the next 7 days, framed as 'validating the business case and ensuring exec alignment before we finalize the proposal'. Without exec engagement, forecast and timeline are at risk.

deltas.to: Reinforce the Coupa wedge in the RFI response and F2F meeting. Coupa is the known active competitor (Mona asked direct commercial comparison 20 May). The buyer'
deltas.subject: Reinforce the Coupa wedge in the RFI response and F2F meeting. Coupa is the known active competitor (Mona asked direct commercial comparison 20 May). The buyer's stated preferences align strongly with Zycus: modular/flexible (vs Coupa rigid), vendor-direct implementation (vs Coupa partner-only, which the buyer dislikes due to cost inflation concerns), configuration capability (vs Coupa zero-customization), and agentic AI leadership (buyer impressed by the relevant stakeholder demo). The RFI response and F2F meeting (overdue, early June window passed) must hammer this wedge: emphasize modular flexibility, vendor-direct implementation, 85-90% fit with configuration, and make the Merlin agentic AI story (guided PR creation, policy compliance automation) the centerpiece. If we don't differentiate sharply from Coupa's rigid model, the buyer may default to brand familiarity.

scope

gaps: Economic Buyer not identified: no buyer participant on 20 May call discussed budget authority or commercial approval. CFO (David Fan) and Head of Strategic Procurement (Jacqueline Lam Chu Wan) are plausible candidates from account contacts but not engaged on this opp. Next Step 6 Jun flags 'need to navigate exec levels asap'.

Evaluation committee unknown: only Decision Maker (Mona) engaged. No other evaluators, IT stakeholders, or finance contacts on opp (Lois W C Ho is Evaluator role but dormant). Shortlist timeline and approval chain not stated on 20 May call.

Paper process unknown: no discussion of contracting, legal review, InfoSec questionnaire, or procurement approval mechanics on 20 May call. Buyer mentioned geopolitical/data sovereignty sensitivity and integration requirements (Oracle, CAMS, Maximo), implying InfoSec and IT review will occur, but no timeline or process stated.

Quantified business case: pain is clear but not quantified. No savings target, cycle-time reduction, error rate, or headcount impact stated on 20 May call. Buyer will need quantified ROI for exec/CFO approval.

Champion advocacy evidence: Decision Maker (Mona) is engaged and positive, but no evidence of active advocacy (exec introductions, internal doc sharing, process coaching, pushing back on competitors) beyond RFI submission. Champion strength is developing, not strong.

Only 2 buyer-side contact roles on this opp (Decision Maker and dormant Evaluator), below multi-thread bar. Add engaged stakeholders (CFO, Head of Strategic Procurement, IT/integration contacts) as they surface.
